title: The Fried Liver Setup
setup: An Italian-style position where the **bishop on c4** points straight at Black's most vulnerable square — **f7**.
hint: When the f7-square is defended only by the king, sacrifice the bishop to drag the monarch into the open.
explanation:
## The Idea

This is the famous **f7 sacrifice** — a thematic blow that exploits Black's weakest square. In the starting position, f7 is defended only by the black king, so a piece landing there forces the king to recapture and step into a draft.

White plays {{Bxf7+}}, sacrificing the bishop.

- {{Kxf7}} is the only legal response: there is no other defender of f7, no piece can interpose on a one-square attack, and the king must address the check.
- After the trade, White has given up a bishop (3) for a pawn (1) — a temporary deficit of two points.

## Why It's Worth It

The king is now stranded on f7, far from safety. In real games this leads to the **Fried Liver Attack** (with Ng5 and Nxf7) or various king hunts where the displaced king proves fatal.

> When Black's king is the *only* defender of f7, look for a sacrifice — even one that loses material on the board. The exposed king is often worth more than a piece.

This is the original "draw the king out" pattern, and it appears in beginner games for centuries to come.
